title: Gas Prices Jump Over 30 Cents in a Week—Here's Why Your Family Road Trip Might Cost More
summary: Have you noticed the numbers at the gas station climbing higher and higher? You're not imagining it. In just one week, the average price for a gallon of regular gas in the U.S. jumped more than 30 cents—from $4.099 to $4.446. That's the highest it's been since July 2022. So what's going on? The main reason is that a very important waterway called the Strait of Hormuz is closed. This narrow passage in the Middle East is like a highway for oil tankers. Because of the war in Iran, ships can't get through, so less oil is reaching the world. When there's less oil, gas prices go up. Kevin Book, an energy expert, says prices could keep rising for weeks or even months. "When you can't get oil out of the strait, you should expect prices to keep rising," he explained. The U.S. government tried to help by releasing millions of barrels of oil from its emergency reserve, but it wasn't enough to stop the spike. President Trump has promised that when the war ends, gas prices will "drop like a rock." But experts warn that even after the strait reopens, it could take a long time for things to get back to normal. Damaged equipment needs fixing, and trapped ships need to move. Plus, if prices do fall fast, it might be because the economy is in trouble—meaning people can't afford to buy as much gas. Higher gas prices don't just hurt at the pump. They also make everything else more expensive, from food to toys, because trucks use fuel to deliver goods. And with the U.S. dollar losing value, traveling abroad costs more too. So for now, maybe that summer road trip will need a little extra planning.
why_it_matters: When gas jumps in price, it costs more to move everything—from the food at your grocery store to the bus that takes you to school, which can stretch your family's budget and change vacation plans.

keywords.explanation: A narrow sea passage in the Middle East where many oil tankers travel, like a busy water highway.
Tankers are enormous ships built to carry huge amounts of oil across the oceans.
Barrels are a unit used to measure large amounts of oil (one barrel equals 42 gallons).
This is a government’s hidden stockpile of extra oil saved for times when supplies are cut off.
The economy is the system of making, buying, and selling things in a country.
The value of a country’s money means how much it can buy compared to other countries’ money.

questions.question: In just one week, how much did the average U.S. gas price jump?
What is the Strait of Hormuz mainly used for?
options: A narrow waterway for oil tankers
A canal that connects two lakes
questions.correct_answer: A narrow waterway for oil tankers

questions.question: What main event caused the gas price spike according to the article?
options: Closure of the Strait of Hormuz due to war in Iran
A hurricane that destroyed oil rigs
A new tax added by the U.S. government
questions.correct_answer: Closure of the Strait of Hormuz due to war in Iran

questions.question: What did the U.S. government do to try to lower gas prices?
options: Released millions of barrels of oil from its emergency reserve
Told all Americans to drive less
Banned selling gas for a month
Asked Saudi Arabia to take over the market
questions.correct_answer: Released millions of barrels of oil from its emergency reserve

questions.question: Who is Kevin Book in the article?
Besides gas, what also becomes more expensive when fuel prices rise?
options: Food and toys because delivery trucks need fuel
Only bus tickets and plane flights
questions.correct_answer: Food and toys because delivery trucks need fuel

background_read: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman that is a major route for oil tankers; if it's blocked, the whole world feels the pinch.

The U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve (the emergency supply mentioned in the article) stores hundreds of millions of barrels of oil in huge underground salt caves in Texas and Louisiana, ready for crises like this.
President Donald Trump (the U.S. president since 2025) has promised to bring gas prices down after the war, but experts say damaged equipment and trapped ships mean it will take a long time to fix.
Article_Structure: WHO: U.S. drivers, the U.S. government, and President Trump
WHAT: Gas prices jumped more than 30 cents in a week because the Strait of Hormuz closed.
WHERE: The trouble started in the Strait of Hormuz in the Middle East, but the price rise hit gas stations across the whole United States.
WHY: A war in Iran blocked oil tankers, so less oil reached the world, causing prices to soar.

perspectives.description: Some people think high prices might push families to use less gas and switch to electric cars, which reduces pollution and helps the planet.
For many families, especially those with less money, more expensive gas means tough choices—cutting back on trips, meals out, or other fun activities.
Energy experts see this as a clear example of how a far-away war can quickly change daily life everywhere, showing that the modern world is tightly linked.
